Our analysis identified three populations of monocytes: ncMos based on expression of FCGR3A and HES4, Mo-1s by expression of FCER1A and Mo-2s via increased expression of CSF3R, FCAR and SELL, compared with Mo-1s (Fig. 4b and Extended Data Fig. 6b-d).

The scRNA-seq analysis also identified a population of human WAT neutrophils based on expression of THBS1 and S100A12, which displayed enrichment for the neutrophil gene module compared with other myeloid cell clusters (Fig. 4b and Extended Data Fig. 6b,c,f).

The scRNA-seq analysis also identified a population of human WAT neutrophils based on expression of THBS1 and S100A12, which displayed enrichment for the neutrophil gene module compared with other myeloid cell clusters (Fig. 4b and Extended Data Fig. 6b,c,f).

We confirmed the presence of two previously identified macrophage subsets present within the human WAT: TREM2, CD9 and LPL expressing lipid-associated macrophages (LAMs)8, LYVE1, SELENOP and C1Q, expressing PVMs32, and an uncharacterized inflammatory macrophage (IM) population with high expression of CCL3L1, TNF and CXCL3 (Fig. 4b and Extended Data Fig. 6b,c).

DC-sorted scRNA-seq analysis identified three distinct WAT DC populations: conventional type 1 DCs (cDC1s) defined by expression of IRF8, DPP4, CADM1 and XCR1, coventional type 2 DCs subset A (cDC2As), via expression of CD1C, IRF4, IL7R and LAMP3, and conventional type 2 DCs subset B (cDC2Bs) by increased expression of CD1C, IRF4, FCER1A and CLEC10A, consistent with the phenotypes of DC subsets recently reported in human blood34 (Fig. 4f, Extended Data Fig. 6b,c and Supplementary Table 4).

DC-sorted scRNA-seq analysis identified three distinct WAT DC populations: conventional type 1 DCs (cDC1s) defined by expression of IRF8, DPP4, CADM1 and XCR1, coventional type 2 DCs subset A (cDC2As), via expression of CD1C, IRF4, IL7R and LAMP3, and conventional type 2 DCs subset B (cDC2Bs) by increased expression of CD1C, IRF4, FCER1A and CLEC10A, consistent with the phenotypes of DC subsets recently reported in human blood34 (Fig. 4f, Extended Data Fig. 6b,c and Supplementary Table 4).

Analysis of the lean interactome suggested that cDC2As may serve an immunoregulatory role via production of growth arrest-specific 6 (GAS6), Jagged1 (JAG1), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF)b and AREG, interacting with APCs, pADs, Endos and SMCs.

Analysis of the lean interactome suggested that cDC2As may serve an immunoregulatory role via production of growth arrest-specific 6 (GAS6), Jagged1 (JAG1), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF)b and AREG, interacting with APCs, pADs, Endos and SMCs.

Analysis of the lean interactome suggested that cDC2As may serve an immunoregulatory role via production of growth arrest-specific 6 (GAS6), Jagged1 (JAG1), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF)b and AREG, interacting with APCs, pADs, Endos and SMCs.

Furthermore, cDC2As uniquely expressed the chemokines CCL17, CCL22 and CCL19, and the cytokine IL-15, which interacted with ILCs, NK cells and APCs, suggesting additional roles for cDC2As in the recruitment and maintenance of innate lymphocytes and progenitor cell types in the lean WAT.

pADs expressed IL-15RA, suggesting a synergistic mechanism with cDC2A-derived IL-15 to regulate WAT lymphocyte populations.

In contrast, PVMs probably play an important role in WAT homeostasis through interactions with structural cells involving vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGFA), transforming growth factor (TGF)b and PDGFC.

In contrast, PVMs probably play an important role in WAT homeostasis through interactions with structural cells involving vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGFA), transforming growth factor (TGF)b and PDGFC.

cDC2Bs expressed MIF, IL-18, IL-1b and TNFSF9, interacting with ILCs, NK cells and myeloid cells, suggesting that DC populations may exacerbate adipose tissue inflammation and tissue fibrosis in a similar manner to proinflammatory macrophages.

cDC2Bs expressed MIF, IL-18, IL-1b and TNFSF9, interacting with ILCs, NK cells and myeloid cells, suggesting that DC populations may exacerbate adipose tissue inflammation and tissue fibrosis in a similar manner to proinflammatory macrophages.

cDC2Bs expressed MIF, IL-18, IL-1b and TNFSF9, interacting with ILCs, NK cells and myeloid cells, suggesting that DC populations may exacerbate adipose tissue inflammation and tissue fibrosis in a similar manner to proinflammatory macrophages.
Similarly, obese ILC3s appear to be mediators of adipose tissue inflammation via TNFSF13B and MIF, acting on macrophage, DC and monocyte subsets.
Similarly, obese ILC3s appear to be mediators of adipose tissue inflammation via TNFSF13B and MIF, acting on macrophage, DC and monocyte subsets.
However, in the obese state, our data suggest that ILC3s are mediators of adipose tissue inflammation via expression of LIF, TNFSF13B and MIF.
Our data also suggest that trNK cells are involved in the recruitment of cDC1s via XCL1/2-XCR1 signaling during obesity, similar to mechanisms recently observed within the tumor microenvironment48.
